threats.title: rentalWallet Excluded from _deriveRentalOrderHash Enables stopRent Wallet Substitution
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveRentalOrderHash function in Signer.sol omits order.rentalWallet from the abi.encode call despite the EIP-712 type string declaring 'address rentalWallet'. This means two RentalOrders identical in all fields except rentalWallet produce the same hash. An attacker calling stopRent can supply a modified rentalWallet while the computed orderHash still matches the stored hash, causing reclamation from the wrong Safe and permanent desync of rentedAssets tracking.
threats.investigation_rationale: The encoding at Signer.sol jumps from order.renter directly to order.startTimestamp, skipping order.rentalWallet. stopRent uses the caller-supplied RentalOrder to compute the hash and perform reclamation. If rentalWallet can be substituted, _reclaimRentedItems operates on the wrong Safe, and rentalAssetUpdates are computed with wrong RentalIds, leaving the original Safe's assets permanently locked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode(..., order.renter, order.startTimestamp, ...) — order.rentalWallet is absent between order.renter and order.startTimestamp despite the type string declaring it

preconditions: Two protocol-deployed rental Safes exist (Safe A and Safe B)
Both Safes hold the same ERC1155 token+id via separate active rentals
Attacker knows the full RentalOrder struct fields for Safe A's rental

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: order.rentalWallet is in the type string but omitted from abi.encode, allowing hash collision across different wallets
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that caller-supplied rentalWallet matches the original rental's wallet

analysis_questions: Can an attacker construct two concurrent rentals with identical fields except rentalWallet to produce matching hashes?
Does the Reclaimer's address(this) == rentalOrder.rentalWallet check prevent reclamation from wrong Safe?
What happens to rentedAssets for the original Safe after the substituted stop?
validation_requirements: Verify the abi.encode in _deriveRentalOrderHash against the type string
Test stopRent with substituted rentalWallet on matching-hash orders
Verify permanent lock of original Safe's assets after wrong-wallet stop

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker rents same ERC1155 token to Safe A and Safe B. Calls stopRent for Safe A's order but substitutes rentalWallet=Safe B. Hash matches. NFTs reclaimed from Safe B, rentedAssets decremented for Safe B's RentalIds. Safe A's rentedAssets remain positive permanently, locking assets in Safe A forever.
prerequisites: Two rental Safes with overlapping ERC1155 token holdings
Knowledge of the full RentalOrder parameters

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent locking of lender's NFTs in original rental Safe; potential theft of NFTs from substitute Safe
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders with ERC1155 rentals, and renters whose Safes are used as substitutes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No on-chain recovery mechanism for permanently locked assets

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test covers stopRent with modified rentalWallet

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Two active rental positions with same ERC1155 token
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction after both rentals created
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Event data would show wrong rentalWallet

threats.title: orderType and emittedExtraData Excluded from _deriveOrderMetadataHash Allows Fulfiller Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveOrderMetadataHash function only encodes rentDuration and hooks into the hash, despite the EIP-712 type string declaring 'OrderMetadata(uint8 orderType,uint256 rentDuration,Hook[] hooks,bytes emittedExtraData)'. This allows the fulfiller to change the orderType field without invalidating the zoneHash, potentially altering payment settlement logic between BASE and PAY orders.
threats.investigation_rationale: The metadata hash is verified against zoneHash in _isValidOrderMetadata. If orderType is not in the hash, the fulfiller can substitute it. While current _convertToItems enforces structural item-type constraints that limit cross-type exploitation, the settlement logic in _settlePayment branches on orderType, and emittedExtraData injection pollutes event logs used by off-chain indexers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode(_ORDER_METADATA_TYPEHASH, metadata.rentDuration, keccak256(...)) — metadata.orderType and keccak256(metadata.emittedExtraData) are absent

preconditions: Fulfiller (renter) controls the RentPayload.metadata struct
A valid CREATE_SIGNER signature exists for the payload

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: EIP-712 hash excludes orderType and emittedExtraData
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Only rentDuration and hooks are hashed; orderType drives settlement logic in PaymentEscrow._settlePayment
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No independent validation that orderType matches lender intent

analysis_questions: Can a BASE→PAY type substitution pass _convertToItems item validation?
Does PAY→BASE substitution change payment flow in _settlePayment?
Can emittedExtraData injection cause off-chain indexer corruption?
validation_requirements: Test orderType substitution with various order configurations
Verify which type transitions pass _convertToItems structural checks
Trace settlement amount differences for substituted orderTypes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Fulfiller intercepts a lender's BASE order and changes orderType to PAY in the metadata. If structural checks pass, the stored order has PAY type. During stopRent, PAY order pro-rata settlement gives the renter a portion of the payment that should have gone entirely to the lender.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Misdirected ERC20 payments between lender and renter; corrupted off-chain event data
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Lenders whose order type semantics are altered
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No on-chain recovery for misdirected payments

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifies orderType is committed in the metadata hash

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Order type mismatch visible in emitted events vs lender intent

threats.title: Guard Allows Non-Standard NFT Functions Enabling Rented Asset Theft via Unguarded Selectors
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard._checkTransaction only blocks a fixed set of known ERC721/ERC1155 function selectors. Any function selector NOT in the if/else chain passes through silently. If a rented NFT implements EIP-4494 permit(), custom approval functions, or any non-standard transfer mechanism, the renter can call these from the rental Safe to grant approvals, then an external address can call transferFrom to extract the NFT without Safe guard intervention.
threats.investigation_rationale: The guard's else branch only checks setApprovalForAll, safeBatchTransferFrom, and setGuard. All other selectors are implicitly allowed. EIP-4494 permit() and similar gasless approval functions set ERC721 approvals without using the standard approve() selector. Once an approval is set, any external address can directly call the NFT contract's transferFrom, which the Safe's guard cannot intercept.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Fixed selector checks with implicit allow for unrecognized selectors; permit(), burn(), and other non-standard selectors pass through

preconditions: Rented NFT implements EIP-4494 permit or other non-standard approval mechanism
The additional function selector is not in the guard's checked set
Renter controls the rental Safe's transaction initiation

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Implicit allow for unknown selectors in else branch
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Only setApprovalForAll, safeBatchTransferFrom, setGuard are blocked in final else; permit(), burn(), and custom functions pass through
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No whitelist-based approach; no catch-all deny for unknown selectors on rented assets

analysis_questions: Which popular NFT collections implement EIP-4494 permit or similar functions?
Can burn() be called to permanently destroy rented assets?
Are there other common non-standard functions that could transfer/approve rented NFTs?
validation_requirements: Test guard behavior with EIP-4494 permit selector from rental Safe
Verify external transferFrom succeeds after permit-based approval
Test burn() call on rented NFT through the guard

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Renter rents an NFT that implements EIP-4494 permit. From the rental Safe, renter calls permit(attacker, tokenId, deadline, v, r, s). Guard does not recognize the selector and allows it. Attacker then directly calls NFT.transferFrom(safe, attacker, tokenId) outside the Safe's guard. NFT is stolen.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete theft of rented NFTs; lender loses asset permanently
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders renting NFTs with non-standard function implementations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — NFT is transferred to attacker's address

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test covers non-standard NFT selectors through the guard

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Two transactions: permit from Safe, then transferFrom externally
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Permit event visible on-chain but may not be monitored

threats.title: EIP-712 _RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH Uses abi.encode Instead of abi.encodePacked for Type String Concatenation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _deriveRentalTypehashes, the rentalOrderTypeHash is computed as keccak256(abi.encode(rentalOrderTypeString, hookTypeString, itemTypeString)) instead of keccak256(abi.encodePacked(...)). Per EIP-712, the struct type hash must be the keccak256 of concatenated type strings. abi.encode adds ABI padding between dynamic bytes arguments, producing a completely different hash from the EIP-712 specification. This makes the on-chain type hash incompatible with any standard EIP-712 implementation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The rentPayloadTypeHash correctly uses abi.encodePacked in the same function, creating an inconsistency. The wrong type hash means that any off-chain system computing hashes per the EIP-712 spec will derive different values. If the protocol ever needs cross-contract or cross-system hash verification of rental orders, the mismatch becomes a breaking issue.

proof_of_signal.pattern: rentalOrderTypeHash = keccak256(abi.encode(rentalOrderTypeString, hookTypeString, itemTypeString)) uses abi.encode while rentPayloadTypeHash at lines 351-355 correctly uses abi.encodePacked

preconditions: Contract is deployed and operational
Any operation deriving rental order hashes uses this incorrect type hash

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: abi.encode used for type string concatenation instead of abi.encodePacked
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: abi.encode adds 32-byte length + offset padding per dynamic bytes, changing the hash entirely from EIP-712 spec
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Internal consistency masks the issue but prevents standard EIP-712 interoperability

analysis_questions: Does any off-chain component depend on standard EIP-712 rental order hash computation?
Could a future integration break when computing the hash per spec?
Could the wrong type hash enable hash collisions that the correct hash would prevent?
validation_requirements: Compare on-chain type hash with spec-compliant keccak256(abi.encodePacked(...)) computation
Verify whether off-chain signer uses same incorrect encoding

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An off-chain system computes the rental order hash per EIP-712 spec and gets a different result. This breaks signature verification, order matching, or any cross-system hash comparison. If the protocol transitions to off-chain order verification, this mismatch becomes a critical interoperability failure.
prerequisites: Any system computing rental order hashes following EIP-712 spec

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Signature verification failure; potential for hash collisions under non-standard encoding
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users if off-chain systems rely on standard EIP-712

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test compares on-chain type hash with EIP-712 spec-compliant computation

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: N/A — code defect present at deployment
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare hash outputs between on-chain and spec-compliant computation

threats.title: EIP-712 Hook Hash Does Not keccak256 Dynamic bytes extraData Field
threats.hypothesis_description: _deriveHookHash passes hook.extraData directly to abi.encode instead of keccak256(hook.extraData) as required by EIP-712 for dynamic 'bytes' types. This produces non-standard hashes that deviate from EIP-712 specification, affecting all hash derivations that include hooks (metadata hash, rental order hash, rent payload hash).
threats.investigation_rationale: EIP-712 requires dynamic types (bytes, string, arrays) to be hashed with keccak256 before encoding. The raw abi.encode of bytes includes offset/length/data encoding, not the keccak256 hash. This affects hook-dependent hashes used in both order creation (metadata verification) and order stopping (order hash verification).

proof_of_signal.pattern: keccak256(abi.encode(_HOOK_TYPEHASH, hook.target, hook.itemIndex, hook.extraData)) — hook.extraData is bytes and should be keccak256(hook.extraData)

preconditions: Any rental order with hooks uses this incorrect encoding
Off-chain signer must use the same non-standard encoding for signatures to verify

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Dynamic bytes type encoded raw instead of keccak256'd
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Non-standard EIP-712 encoding; if off-chain signer follows spec, signatures will never verify on-chain

analysis_questions: Does the off-chain CREATE_SIGNER use the same non-standard encoding?
Could different extraData values produce hash collisions under raw encoding?
Does this affect the uniqueness guarantees of order hashes?
validation_requirements: Compare _deriveHookHash output with EIP-712 compliant computation
Test signature verification with hooks that have varying extraData

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If the protocol signer is updated to use standard EIP-712 encoding for hook hashes, all hook-dependent operations will fail because on-chain and off-chain hashes diverge. Alternatively, under the current non-standard encoding, different extraData values that happen to produce the same ABI encoding could collide.
prerequisites: Hooks are used in rental orders

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Signature verification failures; potential hash collision attacks
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users creating rentals with hooks

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test validates hook hash against EIP-712 spec

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: N/A — code defect present at deployment
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Hash comparison between on-chain and spec-compliant computation

threats.title: Guard Reverts on Calldata Less Than 4 Bytes Trapping ETH in Rental Safes
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard.checkTransaction reverts if data.length < 4, blocking all transactions from the rental Safe that have empty or short calldata. This prevents the Safe owner from sending ETH via native transfers (which use empty calldata). Any ETH received by the rental Safe (e.g., from game rewards, refunds, or accidental sends) becomes permanently trapped.
threats.investigation_rationale: The requirement for data.length >= 4 at Guard.sol line 292 means the Safe cannot execute a simple ETH transfer. Gnosis Safe's execTransaction with empty data and non-zero value is the standard way to send ETH. This path is blocked, creating permanent ETH lock in rental Safes.
preconditions: Rental Safe receives ETH through any mechanism (game rewards, direct sends, refunds)
Safe owner attempts to send ETH with empty calldata

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Minimum 4-byte calldata requirement blocks native ETH transfers
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: ETH has no extraction mechanism from rental Safes
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No special handling for value-only transactions with empty data

analysis_questions: Can ETH be sent from the Safe using any other method (e.g., WETH wrap/transfer)?
Is there a whitelisted delegate or module that could extract ETH?
How likely are rental Safes to receive ETH in practice?
validation_requirements: Test ETH send from rental Safe with empty calldata
Verify that no workaround exists for ETH extraction

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: ETH is sent to a rental Safe (e.g., from a game contract reward). The Safe owner cannot send it out because the guard requires at least 4 bytes of calldata. The ETH remains permanently trapped. In gaming use cases, this could amount to significant trapped value.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of ETH sent to rental Safes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All rental Safe owners who receive ETH

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test covers ETH transfer from rental Safe

threats.title: CEI Violation in stopRent: External Calls Before Storage Removal Enable Reentrancy
threats.hypothesis_description: In Stop.stopRent, external calls (_removeHooks → hook.onStop, _reclaimRentedItems → NFT safeTransferFrom callbacks, ESCRW.settlePayment → ERC20 transfers) all execute BEFORE STORE.removeRentals. During the ERC721/ERC1155 safeTransferFrom callback to the lender, the rental order is still active in storage (orders[hash]=true, rentedAssets still positive). A malicious lender contract could exploit this window.
threats.investigation_rationale: The execution order in stopRent is: hooks → reclaim NFTs → settle payments → remove from storage. ERC721.safeTransferFrom triggers onERC721Received on the lender. At callback time, the rental is fully active in storage. While same-order re-stop would fail (NFTs already transferred), cross-order interactions or state-dependent logic could be exploited.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _removeHooks (external) → _reclaimRentedItems (external NFT callbacks) → ESCRW.settlePayment (external) → STORE.removeRentals (state update) — classic CEI violation

preconditions: Lender is a smart contract implementing onERC721Received/onERC1155Received
Lender contract performs reentrant calls during the callback
No reentrancy guard on stopRent

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External calls (hooks, NFT transfers, ERC20 transfers) before STORE.removeRentals
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Rental is still 'active' in storage during lender's callback
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant modifier; no CEI pattern

analysis_questions: What actions can the lender's callback perform while the rental is still active?
Can stopRentBatch be exploited through cross-order reentrancy?
Does the reentrancy window allow double settlement of ERC20 payments?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious lender contract that reenters during callback
Test cross-order interactions in stopRentBatch during reentrancy
Verify whether double settlement is possible

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Lender contract receives ERC721 via safeTransferFrom callback. During onERC721Received, it calls stopRent for a different order. The second stop fully processes (settle + remove). Back in original execution, ESCRW.settlePayment may interact with altered escrow balances.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential escrow balance manipulation or griefing of batch operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users with orders involving smart contract lenders

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy tests for stopRent with callback-capable lender

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Multiple stopRent events in single tx visible on-chain

threats.title: Hook Status Change After Rental Creation Can Permanently Lock Rental Assets
threats.hypothesis_description: If a GUARD_ADMIN disables a hook's onStop bit (via updateHookStatus) after a rental was created with that hook, stopRent will revert when _removeHooks checks STORE.hookOnStop(target) and finds it disabled. The rental cannot be stopped, permanently locking the lender's NFTs in the renter's Safe and the ERC20 payments in escrow.
threats.investigation_rationale: The hooks array is stored in the RentalOrder struct at creation time and is required to match during stop. _removeHooks at Stop.sol:189 checks hookOnStop(target) which reads the CURRENT hook status. If the status was changed after creation, the check fails, causing an irreversible DoS.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (!STORE.hookOnStop(target)) { revert Errors.Shared_DisabledHook(target); } — checks current hook status, not status at creation time

preconditions: Rental created with hooks that have onStop enabled
GUARD_ADMIN changes hook status to disable onStop after rental creation
Anyone tries to call stopRent for the affected rental

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Runtime hook status check blocks stop if hook was disabled post-creation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No mechanism to skip hooks or force-stop rentals when hooks are disabled
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fallback path when hook status changes; no snapshot of hook status at creation

analysis_questions: Is there any emergency mechanism to bypass hook checks during stop?
Can GUARD_ADMIN re-enable the hook to unblock the stop?
What happens if the hook contract itself is destroyed?
validation_requirements: Create rental with hook, disable hook, attempt stopRent
Verify that re-enabling the hook allows stop to proceed
Test with selfdestruct'd hook contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A rental is created with a hook enabled for onStop. Later, GUARD_ADMIN disables the hook (e.g., due to vulnerability in the hook). All active rentals using that hook become permanently unstoppable. NFTs locked in rental Safes, ERC20 locked in escrow.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent lock of rented NFTs and escrowed payments
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders with active rentals using the disabled hook
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only if GUARD_ADMIN re-enables the hook

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test covers hook status change between rental creation and stop

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Two transactions: disable hook, then attempt stop
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Hook status change visible via events

threats.title: Empty checkAfterExecution Allows Delegatecall to Modify Guard/Module Configuration Undetected
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard.checkAfterExecution is completely empty. When a whitelisted delegatecall target executes in the Safe's context, it can modify arbitrary storage slots including the guard slot (keccak256('guard_manager.guard.address') - 1) or module linked list. Since there is no post-execution verification, the guard and module configuration can be silently removed, allowing all subsequent transactions to bypass security checks.
threats.investigation_rationale: checkTransaction allows delegatecall to whitelisted addresses. A delegatecall executes in the Safe's storage context with full SSTORE capability. checkAfterExecution at Guard.sol:270 is empty, providing no post-execution integrity verification. If a whitelisted contract has any vulnerability allowing arbitrary storage writes, or is intentionally malicious, the guard can be permanently disabled.
deployment_assumptions: At least one address is whitelisted for delegatecall via toggleWhitelistDelegate
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence of specific whitelisted delegates; Admin.toggleWhitelistDelegate exists at Admin.sol

proof_of_signal.pattern: function checkAfterExecution(bytes32 txHash, bool success) external override {} — completely empty body

preconditions: A whitelisted delegatecall target has a vulnerability or is compromised
Safe owner initiates delegatecall to the compromised target
No external monitoring of guard/module state

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Empty checkAfterExecution provides no post-execution integrity verification
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Delegatecall can overwrite guard storage slot, disabling all future protection
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No verification that guard address and module configuration are unchanged after execution

analysis_questions: What is the Gnosis Safe guard storage slot and can a delegatecall write to it?
Can the module linked list be manipulated via delegatecall?
Should checkAfterExecution verify guard and module integrity?
validation_requirements: Deploy a delegatecall target that writes to the guard storage slot
Verify that post-execution the guard is removed
Test subsequent transactions bypass guard after modification

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin whitelists a delegate contract. Safe owner delegatecalls to it. The delegate writes address(0) to the guard storage slot. checkAfterExecution does nothing. All subsequent Safe transactions bypass the guard. Renter transfers all rented NFTs out freely.
prerequisites: Whitelisted delegate with storage write capability

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete theft of all rented NFTs in the Safe
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders with assets in the affected Safe

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifies post-execution integrity of guard/module state

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Active rental in the targeted Safe
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction to disable guard, then arbitrary transfers
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Guard removal visible via Safe guard storage read

threats.title: Active Hook for Target Completely Bypasses Guard _checkTransaction Security Checks
threats.hypothesis_description: When a hook is registered and active for a target contract (contractToHook returns non-zero and hookOnTransaction is true), Guard.checkTransaction forwards the entire transaction to the hook's onTransaction function and completely skips _checkTransaction. If the hook does not replicate all guard protections (transfer blocking, module protection, guard protection), the Safe owner can execute transactions that _checkTransaction would have blocked.
threats.investigation_rationale: At Guard.sol lines 296-303, the if/else structure means _forwardToHook and _checkTransaction are mutually exclusive. A hook registered for a token contract address would receive all calls to that contract, but if it doesn't block transferFrom or approve, rented assets can be transferred out. This delegates full security responsibility to the hook author.
deployment_assumptions: GUARD_ADMIN has configured a hook for a contract that holds rented assets
threats.config_evidence: Guard.updateHookPath and updateHookStatus at Guard.sol; Storage._contractToHook mapping

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (hook != address(0) && isActive) { _forwardToHook(...); } else { _checkTransaction(...); } — mutually exclusive branches

preconditions: GUARD_ADMIN sets a hook for a target contract address
The hook's onTransaction does not enforce transfer/approval blocking
Rented assets are tokens at the hooked target address

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Hook completely replaces _checkTransaction — no layered security
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A buggy or incomplete hook allows operations that _checkTransaction would block
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No base security checks applied when hooks are active

analysis_questions: Should _checkTransaction be called IN ADDITION to the hook, not instead of it?
What minimum security guarantees must a hook enforce?
Can hooks be set for NFT contract addresses, enabling transfer bypass?
validation_requirements: Deploy a hook that allows transferFrom, set it for an NFT contract
Verify that rented NFTs can be transferred out via the hooked path
Test both hook-active and hook-inactive paths

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: GUARD_ADMIN sets a hook for an NFT contract (e.g., for game integration). The hook allows transferFrom calls through. Renter calls transferFrom on the rented NFT from the Safe. Guard forwards to the hook, which doesn't block it. _checkTransaction is never called. NFT is stolen.
prerequisites: Hook set for NFT contract address

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of all rented NFTs at the hooked contract address
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders with assets rented through hooked contracts

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifies guard base checks still apply when hook is active

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Active rental with hooked NFT contract
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Transfer event visible but hook may not emit blocking events

threats.title: Immutable Domain Separator Enables Cross-Chain Signature Replay After Chain Fork
threats.hypothesis_description: The EIP-712 domain separator in Signer.sol is computed at deployment as an immutable value using block.chainid. The _CHAIN_ID is stored but never checked against block.chainid at runtime. After a chain fork, the domain separator remains valid on both chains, enabling cross-chain replay of CREATE_SIGNER signatures to create unauthorized rentals on the forked chain.
threats.investigation_rationale: _recoverSignerFromPayload at Signer.sol uses _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR directly without comparing _CHAIN_ID to block.chainid. Standard EIP-712 implementations (e.g., OpenZeppelin) recompute the separator if the chain ID has changed. The protocol targets multiple chains (Ethereum, Polygon, Avalanche), increasing fork exposure.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _CHAIN_ID stored as immutable at deployment but never compared to block.chainid during signature verification at line 107

preconditions: A chain fork occurs after deployment
The protocol signer's address exists on both chains
Valid signatures from the original chain are available on the forked chain

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Immutable domain separator without runtime chainId validation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _CHAIN_ID is stored but never compared to block.chainid
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No dynamic domain separator recomputation like OpenZeppelin's _domainSeparatorV4()

analysis_questions: Would a chain fork create duplicate protocol deployments?
Could fork-replayed signatures cause meaningful damage?
Does the nonce/expiration mechanism mitigate replay risk?
validation_requirements: Simulate chain fork and verify signature validity on both chains
Check if expiration timestamps provide sufficient protection

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After a chain fork, an attacker replays a valid CREATE_SIGNER signature from the original chain on the forked chain. If the Seaport order and rental safe state are similar on both chains, the rental creation succeeds on the fork, potentially locking assets or draining escrow.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unauthorized rental creation on forked chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol users on the forked chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Would require protocol migration on forked chain

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test simulates chain fork scenario

threats.title: Pro-Rata Calculation Overflow DoS Prevents Early Stop of PAY Orders
threats.hypothesis_description: In PaymentEscrow._calculatePaymentProRata, the expression `(amount * elapsedTime) * 1000` can overflow for large ERC20 amounts. With Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic, this overflow causes a revert, preventing pro-rata settlement and thus blocking early termination of PAY orders. The lender must wait until full expiry to stop the rental.
threats.investigation_rationale: uint256 max is ~1.15e77. For a token with 18 decimals, amount of 1e30 (1 trillion tokens), elapsedTime of 1e7 seconds (~115 days), and multiplied by 1000 gives ~1e40, which is safe. But for amount of 1e59 with any elapsedTime > 1, overflow occurs. While extreme, worthless ERC20 tokens with 18 decimals could be used in PAY orders with astronomical amounts to grief lenders.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 numerator = (amount * elapsedTime) * 1000; — triple multiplication without overflow protection

preconditions: PAY order with very large ERC20 amount (possible with worthless tokens)
Lender attempts to stop the rental before expiry (triggers pro-rata)
amount * elapsedTime * 1000 exceeds uint256 max

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked triple multiplication can overflow for large amounts
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Overflow causes revert in checked Solidity 0.8, blocking pro-rata settlement
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No overflow-safe math or amount bounds check

analysis_questions: What is the minimum ERC20 amount needed to trigger overflow for typical rental durations?
Can the protocol signer prevent large-amount orders?
Does the full-expiry settlement path avoid the pro-rata calculation?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact overflow threshold for various rental durations
Test settlePayment with overflow-triggering amounts
Verify that waiting until expiry allows settlement via the non-pro-rata path

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates a PAY order with a worthless ERC20 at maximum uint256 amount. Lender cannot stop the rental early because _calculatePaymentProRata overflows. Lender's NFTs are locked until the rental expires, potentially for a very long duration.
prerequisites: PAY order with very large ERC20 amount

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Lender's NFTs locked for full rental duration; opportunity cost
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Lenders in PAY orders with large-amount tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Assets recoverable after rental expiry via non-pro-rata path

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test covers large-amount pro-rata calculation
